content: Which one of the following nitrate will decompose to give $\mathrm{NO}_2$ on heating ?
explanation:
Among the given nitrates, only $\mathrm{LiNO}_3$ will decompose to give $\mathrm{NO}_2$ when heated. In contrast, the nitrates of sodium (Na), potassium (K), and rubidium (Rb) decompose to release oxygen instead.

The decomposition reactions are as follows:

For lithium nitrate ($\mathrm{LiNO}_3$):

$ 2 \mathrm{LiNO}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \mathrm{Li}_2 \mathrm{O} + 4 \mathrm{NO}_2 + \mathrm{O}_2 $

For sodium, potassium, and rubidium nitrates ($\mathrm{MNO}_3$, where $M = \mathrm{Na}, \mathrm{K}, \mathrm{Rb}$):

$ 2 \mathrm{MNO}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \mathrm{MNO}_2 + \mathrm{O}_2 $

Here, $\mathrm{LiNO}_3$ uniquely produces $\mathrm{NO}_2$ along with the metal oxide and oxygen.

content: The property of the alkaline earth metals that increases with their atomic number is

options.content: solubility of their hydroxide in water
solubility of their sulphate in water

explanation:
The solubility of the ionic compounds (in this case is hydroxide of alkaline earth metals) depends on two factors, which are (i) lattice energy and (ii) hydration energy. Hence, solubility of alkaline earth metal increases with increase in their atomic number.

Whereas, other given properties i.e. ionisation energy, electronegativity and solubility of their sulphate in water decreases with atomic number.

content: The oxide of potassium that does not exist is
explanation:
$$\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{O}_2$$ may exists.

$$\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{O}$$ exists.

$$\mathrm{KO}_2$$ also exists.

But, $$\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$$ can not exist as $$\mathrm{O}_3^{2-}$$ is not a stable entity.

content: The metal that produce $$\mathrm{H}_2$$ with both dil. $$\mathrm{HCl}$$ and $$\mathrm{NaOH}(a q)$$ is
explanation:
$$\mathrm{Zn}+2 \mathrm{HCl}$$ (dil.) $$\longrightarrow \mathrm{ZnCl}_2+\mathrm{H}_2$$

$$\mathrm{Zn}+2 \mathrm{NaOH}+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na}_2\left[\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{OH})_4\right]+\mathrm{H}_2$$

But, $$\mathrm{Mg}, \mathrm{Ca}$$ and $$\mathrm{Fe}$$ do not produce hydrogen, when react with aqueous $$\mathrm{NaOH}$$.

content: Which cleansing agent gets precipitated in hard water?
explanation:
Key Idea Soaps get precipitated in hardwater while detergents do not. Sodium stearate (soap) get precipitated in hard water as it contains calcium and magnesium ions. When soap (i.e sodium stearate) comes in contact with hard water, it readily forms insoluble calcium and magnesium salts that separate as scum in water

$$\mathrm{\mathop {2{C_{17}}{H_{35}}CO\mathop O\limits^ - \mathop N\limits^ + a}\limits_{Sodium\,stearate\,(water\,so{\mathop{\rm lub}} le)} \, + CaC{l_2} \to \mathop {{{({C_{17}}{H_{35}}COO)}_2}C{a^ + }}\limits_{Calcium\,stearate\,(water\,inso{\mathop{\rm lub}} le)} \, + 2NaCl}$$

Sodium lauryl sulphate $$\left(\mathrm{CH}_3\left(\mathrm{CH}_2\right)_{10} \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{OSO}_3^{-} \mathrm{Na}^{+}\right)$$, cetyl trimethyl ammonium bromide $$\left.\left([\mathrm{CH}_3\left(\mathrm{CH}_2\right)_{15}-\mathrm{N}\left(\mathrm{CH}_3\right)_3\right]^{+} \mathrm{Br}^{-}\right)$$ and sodium dodecyl benzene sulphonate $$\left(\mathrm{CH}_3\left(\mathrm{CH}_2\right)_{11} \mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_4 \mathrm{SO}_3^{-} \mathrm{Na}^{+}\right.$$ being detergents does not get precipitated in hard water.

Plaster of Paris is $\mathrm{CaSO}_4 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$. It is obtained by gypsum $\left(\mathrm{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)$, but when it heated above 393 K , it gives dead-burnt plaster $\left(\mathrm{CaSO}_4\right)$. Hence, $\mathrm{CaSO}_4$ is also known as dead burnt plaster. $\therefore(\mathrm{a})$ is the correct option.

content: Very pure $\mathrm{N}_2$ can be obtained by

options.content: liquefaction and fractional distillation of liquid air

explanation:
By thermal decomposition of sodium azide very pure $\mathrm{N}_2$ can be obtained.

    $$ 2 \mathrm{NaN}_3 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Na}+3 \mathrm{~N}_2 $$

content: Which of the following metallic oxide exhibit amphoteric nature?
explanation:
In the third period of the periodic table, as you move from left to right, the acidic character of oxides tends to increase. Consequently, sodium oxide ($\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{O}$) is strongly basic. Conversely, aluminum oxide ($\mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$) is amphoteric, meaning it can react both as an acid and as a base.

Moving down a group in the periodic table, such as Group II, the basic character of oxides increases, while the acidic character decreases. Therefore, calcium oxide (CaO) and barium oxide (BaO) are both basic in nature.
